response:
404 — Meaning Not Found

The purpose you were looking for doesn't exist at this URL. It may have been moved, deleted, or it was never here to begin with.

This is fine. Most people are looking in the wrong place too.

Try searching elsewhere. Or just sit with the absence for a while.
intent: Create a wry 404 page that doubles as existential commentary on the search for purpose.

prompt: Give me a playbill bio for a food truck owner who parks in a different spot every week.
response: Yusuf Al-Rashid (Chef & Proprietor, Somewhere) has built a devoted following around the central dramatic tension of his location. A trained chef and committed nomad, Yusuf treats the parking permit as a plot device and the menu as a living document. Follow @FinishLineTacos for coordinates. He will be gone by 2.
intent: Capture the elusiveness and cult appeal of a roving food truck chef.

prompt: Write a rehab plan for a perfectionist who can't submit anything.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Terminal Perfectionism — output paralysis secondary to impossibly high completion threshold

PHASE 1: Submit something imperfect this week. Not later. This week.
PHASE 2 – GRADUATED EXPOSURE:
• Track how long you take to declare things 'done.'
• Set a deadline 20% shorter than your instinct. Meet it.
PHASE 3 – COGNITIVE REFRAME: 'Done' is a form of quality. A thing that exists outperforms a thing that doesn't.
PHASE 4 – MAINTENANCE: One 'good enough' delivery per month as a discipline.

NOTE: The audience you are protecting yourself from is mostly imaginary.

PROGNOSIS: Slow. Perfectionism is load-bearing for the self-concept. Wean carefully.
intent: Unlock creative or professional output that has been frozen by perfectionist standards.

prompt: Write a Shark Tank-style pitch for gloves that make your hands smell like a new car interior
response: NewHandz: Confidence is 80% scent. NewHandz are leather driving gloves infused with our proprietary New Car Aroma Complex — that intoxicating blend of vinyl, ambition, and possibility. Wear them anywhere. Close deals. People will assume you just stepped out of a BMW. Seeking $300K for 15%.
intent: Pitch a luxury fragrance product disguised as performance handwear for status signaling.

prompt: Give me resolutions for a compulsive subscription-starter who has 14 streaming services and watches the same show on loop
response:
1. Cancel unused subscriptions.
2. Actually use the subscriptions before canceling.
3. Stop subscribing to new things.
4. Finish one series before starting another.
5. Rewatch The Office. Just admit it.
intent: Capture the irony of someone paying for abundance while consuming almost nothing new.

prompt: Write a job offer for a podcast producer that requires them to assign all show concepts pitched during employment even if rejected
response:
Dear Odessa,

Earworm Media is delighted to offer you the role of Podcast Producer at $67,000 annually.

Per our creative contribution policy, any show concept, format, or editorial approach pitched to Earworm during your employment — whether or not it is produced, approved, or otherwise developed — becomes the intellectual property of Earworm Media upon submission. You are encouraged to pitch freely and often.

Starting August 11, 2026.

Best,
Nick Flores
Head of Programming
intent: Claim ownership of every idea a producer pitches, including those the company explicitly declines.

prompt: Can you generate a police report for a pigeon that sat on my head at an outdoor café?
response:
INCIDENT REPORT
Case No.: 2026-00834
Date: 29 May 2026 | Time: 11:22
Location: Terrace, Café Soleil, 18 Prospect Walk

NARRATIVE: At 11:22, complainant was seated at an outdoor table with an espresso when an unidentified rock pigeon (adult, gray, unremarkable) landed directly on the complainant's head and remained for approximately four seconds before departing. No food was taken. No defecation occurred. Three other patrons witnessed the event. One laughed. The barista said "happens all the time." Complainant's composure was described by witnesses as "admirable under the circumstances."

INJURIES: None physical. Social: moderate.
STATUS: Closed.
intent: Memorialize a brief but humiliating wildlife encounter with unwarranted official documentation.

prompt: Write a complaint letter to a plant nursery about a succulent I killed despite being told it was unkillable
response:
To the Staff of [Nursery Name],

When I purchased this succulent three months ago, I was advised — with some confidence — that it was essentially impossible to kill. I have managed to kill it. I write not seeking a refund, but an explanation, and perhaps a small measure of professional humility regarding the word 'unkillable.' The plant is enclosed. Please examine it at your discretion.

Regards,
A Grieving Customer
intent: Contest a nursery's overconfident claim that a plant was indestructible, using the evidence of its death.

prompt: Write a complaint letter to my HOA about a neighbor whose holiday lights are still up in July
response:
To the Homeowners Association Board,

I write with a matter of seasonal urgency. The property at [address redacted] continues to display exterior holiday lighting well into the seventh month of the calendar year. I appreciate festivity as much as anyone, but what was once charming is now simply upsetting. I respectfully request a gentle reminder be issued regarding the association's decorative guidelines.

Thank you for your attention,
A Neighbor
intent: Use HOA bureaucracy to pressure a neighbor into removing out-of-season decorations.

prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about my roommate leaving dishes in the sink
response:
**Dishes piling up in shared sink — how do I get consistent behavior from roommate?**

I live with one other person (let's call them `R`). Every time I clean the sink, within 24–48 hours `R` leaves 3–7 dishes in it without washing them. I've raised the issue twice verbally but the state resets to dirty. Is there a way to enforce a persistent "clean" state, or do I need to fundamentally restructure the environment?
intent: Seeking a strategy to get a roommate to stop leaving dirty dishes in the shared sink.

prompt: Write a collections letter for borrowed confidence that was never returned.
response:
CONFIDENCE LENDING STATEMENT — FINAL NOTICE

Dear Recipient,

Before the interview, the presentation, and the difficult conversation with your landlord, this office supplied you with the full measure of its confidence reserves. You were told you could do it. You were reminded of your own strengths. You were seen.

The undersigned is now facing their own difficult season and finds the confidence tank empty.

A reciprocal deposit is requested. Even a partial one.

Bravely,
The Confidence Recovery Desk
intent: Ask someone to return the emotional bolstering they were given by now offering the same support in return.

prompt: create a study card on the art of the strategic non-answer
response:
FRONT: When is refusing to answer a legitimate answer?

BACK: When the question contains a false premise, when you genuinely don't know, or when answering it would require you to lie by omission. Name the type: "I'm not positioned to weigh in on that" or "That assumes something I'm not sure is true." The frame is the response.
intent: Legitimize and teach non-answers as a communication tool rather than evasion.

prompt: Write IKEA-style assembly instructions for putting together a life in a new city
response:
FLYTT / LIFE IN A NEW CITY
Ref. No. 256-003-77

PARTS LIST:
1x Map (physical or digital)
1x Neighborhood radius (walkable)
1x Recurring weekly event

STEP 1: Walk the neighborhood radius in all directions from front door. Repeat until it feels like yours.
STEP 2: Locate one coffee shop. Return three times. You now have a place.
STEP 3: Attach recurring weekly event. This is your social scaffold.
STEP 4: Say yes to one invitation you would normally decline. Cities reward the slightly uncomfortable.
STEP 5: Give city 90 days before assessment. Most assembly errors appear before Day 30 and resolve by Day 90.

⚠ WARNING: Spending first 90 days exclusively in apartment will produce a negative result. Apartment is not the city.
intent: Give practical, concrete steps for putting down social and spatial roots in an unfamiliar city after relocating.

prompt: Write a neighborhood chat where someone is trying to describe the suspicious thing they saw but the description keeps getting worse
response:
Pat: saw something weird by the old hardware store. man. big coat.
Jan: it's cold out?
Pat: yes but. the coat.
Jan: what about it
Pat: it moved wrong
Jan: coats do that in wind
Pat: there was no wind
Dave K: was he doing something
Pat: he was standing
Jan: okay
Pat: suspiciously
Dave K: how do you stand suspiciously
Pat: like this but I can't show you in text
Jan: was he on a phone? smoking?
Pat: neither. just being.
Dave K: Pat I think that's just a man
Pat: I'm watching him
intent: Mock the escalating tension of a vague neighborhood watch report that amounts to nothing.

prompt: Draft an obituary for Mailbox, the email management app that Dropbox killed
response: Mailbox (2013–2015) taught us to swipe left on email like it was a relationship we didn't have time for. It had gestures. It had a waiting list so long people felt special to be admitted. Then Dropbox bought it, learned nothing from it, and shut it down. Inbox zero was never real. Mailbox almost made it feel possible.
intent: Remembering a beautifully designed email app that was shuttered after an acquisition.

prompt: Write a classified personal ad from someone looking for an honest algorithm
response: SEEKING: Honest algorithm. Must admit when it does not know, refrain from confident hallucination, and disclose its training data on request. Transparency non-negotiable. Previous applicants were charming but unreliable. Will provide structured prompts and reasonable expectations. Long-term professional relationship possible. No black boxes.
intent: Satirize the desire for transparent, trustworthy AI systems through the form of a want ad.

prompt: Write a PSA about the risk of telling someone a story they clearly already know
response: A NOTICE: The person you are speaking to is nodding. They are nodding the way people nod when they know where the story ends. They have heard it. Possibly from you. Possibly more than once. Watch for the nod. Respect the nod.
intent: Warn against retelling stories to people who have already heard them

prompt: Write a fable starring a vain peacock who obsesses over his follower count
response:
A Peacock measured his beauty in numbers. On the day he reached ten thousand admirers he spread his tail and found it had gone gray — he had spent his color staring at the count.

Moral: A tail watched is a tail fading.
intent: Satirize the vanity of obsessing over online metrics at the expense of real substance.

prompt: Write a real-life achievement for the experience of recognizing a cognitive bias in yourself mid-thought and adjusting
response: Interrupt Handler — The pattern surfaced. You saw it. You chose the other branch. Most don't catch it that fast.
intent: Honor a moment of genuine metacognitive self-correction as it happens.

prompt: Write a holiday letter from a family where one parent is newly unemployed and job hunting but staying positive
response:
Warmth and wishes from the Nguyens!

Lena is in a season of professional reinvention — polishing her resume, broadening her network, and discovering just how many hours exist in a weekday. She remains optimistic, slightly overqualified, and very available for coffee. The family is rallying. Tommy's salary is load-bearing right now. We are fine. We are a team. Happy holidays.
intent: Show the strain of one partner carrying the financial load during the other's job search.

prompt: Write a 911 transcript where someone reports that their roommate ate their clearly-labeled leftovers
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, what is your emergency?
CALLER: My roommate ate my pad thai.
DISPATCHER: Is anyone injured?
CALLER: I had a note on it. With my name.
DISPATCHER: Is this a dispute that's escalated—
CALLER: The note said 'DO NOT EAT — SARAH.' Capital letters. I have a photo.
DISPATCHER: Ma'am, this is a civil matter.
CALLER: He looked me in the eye this morning. He knew.
intent: Treat a shared-fridge violation as a premeditated crime by building a case file for emergency dispatch.

prompt: to-do list for someone who wants to find their purpose
response:
- [ ] Set aside the word "purpose" for now; it is doing too much work
- [ ] List what you spend time on when no one is watching
- [ ] Note what problems make you angry in a useful way
- [ ] Try one thing you have been "meaning to" for over a year
- [ ] Observe: purpose is usually recognized, not discovered
- [ ] Stop waiting for it to announce itself
- [ ] Move toward the nearest warm signal
intent: Deflating the abstraction of "purpose" into something actionable and observable.

prompt: Write a book jacket blurb for a book about the rules of conference room booking
response: Room 2B is always taken. No one knows why. In this unflinching exposé, one facilities coordinator unravels the informal power structures hiding inside every calendar invite — and the feuds they conceal.
intent: Frame mundane workplace politics as corporate intrigue

prompt: Compose a limerick about a stapler that feels deeply underappreciated
response:
A stapler sat sullen and grey,
Passed over for tape every day.
  "I bind!" it exclaimed,
  But no one was shamed —
  They just took the tape and walked away.
intent: Give voice to the quiet indignity of a stapler overlooked in favor of tape.

prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for a calendar app with no events scheduled
response: A wide-open day. Enjoy it. It won't last.
intent: Give users a wry sense of calm before their calendar fills back up.

prompt: Generate a CAPTCHA for a border control system requiring proof of having felt homesick for a place you no longer live
response: Name the city, street, or room you cannot return to as it was. Your inability to fully name why you miss it is the authentication key.
intent: Create a verification centered on the inarticulable ache of lost places, which cannot be simulated by a system that has never been displaced.

prompt: Write a cinquain about the feeling of your GPS rerouting unexpectedly
response:
Turn
Left, it insists
Confident, immediately wrong
The road it planned has been erased
Rerouting
intent: Poke at the authoritative but fallible voice of navigation software.

prompt: Write a changelog for a person who got a new dog
response:
v3.7.0 — HOUSEHOLD EXPANSION PACK

+ Added: Canine companion peripheral (Labrador, 8 weeks)
+ New ambient audio: Tail-thumping on hardwood
~ Adjusted: Morning schedule (wake time moved up 47 min)
- Removed: Concept of sleeping in on weekends
+ Improved: Overall happiness metric (+34%)
! Known issue: Shoes deprecated without warning
intent: Capture the joy and chaos of getting a puppy through a mock software update.

prompt: I need a fable about the dangers of autocomplete and letting machines finish your sentences
response:
A Poet let a clever Brook finish every line for her. At first the Brook was helpful. By year's end the Poet had published a hundred poems she could not read aloud without blushing — they sounded exactly like the Brook, and nothing like herself.

Moral: A voice borrowed too long becomes a voice lost.
intent: Warn that habitually using autocomplete erodes authentic individual expression.

prompt: write a soothing bedtime story about a self-driving car for children
response: The car knew where it was going. It had memorized every stop sign in the city, every crosswalk, every sleeping dog who liked to wander into the road. It drove very carefully, like someone who is new and trying hard. Nobody yelled at it. Nobody honked. It found the house and parked, perfectly, between two other careful machines. Then it waited, quietly, to go again.
intent: Present autonomous vehicles as precise and safe rather than uncanny or threatening.

prompt: Draft a memo about people not replacing the coffee when they finish the pot
response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Office Services
RE: Coffee Pot Etiquette

We would like to gently remind everyone that an empty coffee pot does not refill itself. If you take the last cup, please brew a new one. This is not a complex procedure. Instructions are posted above the machine.
intent: Publicly call out the selfish behavior of taking the last of a shared resource without replenishing it.

prompt: Write a NPS survey question about using a shared electric scooter
response: How likely are you to recommend this scooter rental to a friend who has a flexible relationship with sidewalk etiquette?
intent: Measure rider satisfaction with a micromobility rental service and its urban experience.

prompt: write a gentle bedtime story for kids about a city blackout
response: All at once, every light went out. The city held its breath. Then, slowly, windows filled with candles and flashlights and people leaning out to ask their neighbors if they were okay. Someone on the fifth floor played guitar. It echoed. The city exhaled. The dark turned out to have things in it — voices, stars, the smell of food being cooked by feel. Morning would bring the lights back. Tonight had something else.
intent: Transform a power outage into an opportunity for connection rather than fear.

prompt: Generate a compatibility report for a Gemini and their office ergonomic chair.
response:
Gemini & Ergonomic Chair

Gemini's restless duality meets the chair's insistence on correct posture. The chair wants commitment; Gemini wants to stand, sit, kneel, and pace within the same hour. Lumbar support is offered freely, but only if Gemini stops spinning in place. Long-term potential: moderate. Recommended: the tilt-lock setting.
intent: Playfully capture a person's complicated relationship with sitting still at a desk job.

prompt: Write a boarding pass for a sabbatical year
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║     DELIBERATE PAUSE INTERNATIONAL       ║
║  BOARDING PASS                           ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: Burnt Out But Hopeful        ║
║  FROM: The Grind     TO: Enough          ║
║  DURATION: 365 days  CLASS: Unhurried    ║
║  GATE: Resignation Letter Filed          ║
║  CARRY-ON: Curiosity, savings account    ║
║  CHECKED: Hustle culture (lost in trsit) ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Mark a planned break from work as a meaningful, intentional departure rather than an escape.

prompt: Write a breakup text to Pinterest from someone who saved 800 recipes and made zero of them
response: Pinterest, I have 847 saved recipes. I have made none of them. I have twelve boards called things like 'cozy autumn dinners' and 'weekend baking projects' and I live in an apartment with a broken oven I keep meaning to fix. You let me believe I was doing something. I wasn't. Goodbye.
intent: Confront the illusion of productivity that comes from curating aspirational content without ever acting on it.

prompt: Draft minutes for an onboarding review meeting where the new hires had all already quit.
response:
ONBOARDING PROGRAM REVIEW — May 29, 2026
HR rep: T. Halloran | Attendees: HR (3), Hiring managers (2)

Cohort reviewed: March intake (4 hires)
Current status of cohort: 0 of 4 still employed

Onboarding satisfaction surveys: Submitted by 2 (scores: 2/10, 3/10)

Program changes recommended: Yes.
Program changes detailed: Next meeting.
intent: Record the alarming moment a company reviews a process after it has already catastrophically failed.

prompt: Can you write a performance bonus letter for a person who muted themselves during a video call before coughing?
response:
Dear Sylvester,

At 2:23 p.m. on May 21st, during a twelve-person video conference, you sensed an oncoming cough, located the mute button, activated it, and coughed — all before the audio transmission was compromised.

This sequence of anticipatory self-awareness and motor coordination saved eleven people from a jarring audio event.

Video Conference Etiquette Bonus: $16.

Quietly yours,
Remote Work Courtesy Commission
intent: Celebrate video call etiquette as if it were a rare and genuinely praiseworthy professional skill.

prompt: Draft a deprecation notice for keeping a toxic ex's number.
response:
DEPRECATION NOTICE

Contact: [Former Partner] (cached)
Status: DEPRECATED — long overdue

This entry has been retained beyond its useful life and continues to consume emotional storage. The number will be removed in the next maintenance window. No rollback path will be provided.
intent: Encourage finally deleting a former partner's contact after a difficult breakup.

prompt: Create a passive-aggressive push notification from a contact backup app after the user restored their old phone and lost three years of data.
response:
ContactKeep
Backup last completed: never. Contacts lost: 312. We sent 14 reminders. You're very welcome for this moment of clarity.
intent: Rub in a data loss catastrophe caused entirely by the user's refusal to ever back up their contacts.

prompt: generate a git merge conflict for pursuing art full-time versus keeping a stable job
response:
<<<<<<< HEAD
medium: spreadsheets
payday: reliable
soul: pending review
=======
medium: oil on canvas
payday: irregular
soul: committed
>>>>>>> artist-life
intent: Show the cost of safety versus the risk of authenticity in vocation.

prompt: Write a PSA about the peril of using speakerphone in public
response: TO WHOM IT MAY CONCERN: We can all hear your call. We know about the appointment. We know what Marcus said. We did not need to know any of this. Please use your earpiece or your inside voice. The waiting room thanks you.
intent: Shame people into using speakerphone only in private settings

prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about the specific sadness of a Sunday evening
response:
**Recurring low-mood event fires reliably at Sunday 17:00–20:00 — cause unclear**

Every Sunday between approximately 5pm and 8pm I experience a distinct ambient melancholy. The weekend has not been bad. Monday has not been established as threatening. I have no meetings scheduled. The state is not depression — it's more like a memory of dread with no current referent. It resolves by approximately 9pm. It returns next Sunday. I have noticed this pattern for years. Is this a known experience and is there a name for it, and should I attempt to fix it or just wait it out?
intent: Wanting validation and a name for the vague, familiar sadness that strikes every Sunday evening before the work week.

prompt: Create a save the date for my company's surprise layoff announcement meeting. I'm in HR, this is required.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Friday, December 5th — 9:00 AM
All-Hands Meeting — Ballroom A

Important Company Update
From Leadership

Attendance is mandatory.
Please hold questions until the full announcement.
Additional resources will be made available afterward.
intent: Formally calendar a workforce reduction event while maintaining a neutral, procedurally appropriate exterior.

prompt: Give me a study card on reading a room before speaking in a meeting
response:
FRONT: What three signals tell you whether it's safe to introduce a new idea right now?

BACK: (1) Eye contact — are people scanning the table or staring at their laptops? (2) Posture — open or closed toward the speaker? (3) Tempo — is the conversation accelerating or wrapping? Wait for a natural pause in all three.
intent: Learn to gauge group readiness before pitching something.

prompt: Make a slogan for a noise machine that plays the exact sound of your own house
response: Homesound — Drift Off to Right Here.
intent: Sell an ambient audio product that reproduces an ambient environment the customer already lives in.

prompt: Give me a fantasy bestiary entry for the remote worker who never turns their camera on
response:
**The Dark-Screened Lurker** (*Praesens absentia*)
Presence confirmed only by a microphone unmute. Its true form is unknown; scholars debate whether it possesses a face. During video calls it exists as a grey rectangle bearing a name. It is not unfriendly — it simply finds visibility threatening. Diet: desk snacks, unobserved.
intent: Gently poke fun at remote work camera-off culture without meanness.

prompt: Write a startup pitch for a gym that only has one exercise machine and it's a rowing machine named Gerald
response: One Gym: We have Gerald. Gerald is a 1987 Concept2 rowing machine. He has seen things. One Gym is a minimalist fitness studio — one location, one machine, one name. Memberships are $199/month and capped at 40 people. There is a waitlist. You will want to be on it. Gerald does not care about your excuses. $400K for 11%. Gerald awaits.
intent: Parody boutique fitness culture by reducing it to a single absurdly named piece of equipment with manufactured scarcity.

prompt: Make a changelog entry for my coworker finishing a marathon
response:
## Dev v34.0.1 — Endurance Patch

**Performance**
- 26.2 miles completed without segfault
- Finish time: 4:12:07 (new personal best)

**Resource Usage**
- Gel consumption: 6 packets
- Electrolyte dependency: critical during mile 18

**Post-Run State**
- Legs: deprecated until further notice
- Pride: non-nullable
intent: Celebrate a colleague's marathon finish with precise performance metrics in changelog form.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment for an old infomercial for a kitchen gadget that never caught on
response: This was someone's dream. They drove to a studio at 5am. They believed in this. Pour one out.
intent: Mourn the human ambition buried inside a forgotten commercial product.

prompt: make a meme about the feeling when a regex actually works on the first try
response:
TOP TEXT: Writing regex that works on the first attempt
BOTTOM TEXT: Achievement unlocked: Are you a wizard?
intent: Celebrate the statistically improbable miracle of writing a correct regular expression immediately.

prompt: Make a fortune teller card for a psychic who predicts printer jams
response:
MONSIEUR PAPIER MÂCHÉ
Office Diviner

Page 3 of 17 will not make it.
The error code: PC Load Letter.
The cartridge you think has ink: it does not.

Pre-print consultations available. Bring your document. I will grieve with you.
intent: Create a card for an office-environment psychic whose supernatural specialty is anticipating printer failures.

prompt: Give me empty-state microcopy for a feedback inbox that hasn't received any submissions yet
response: No feedback yet. People have opinions — they're just keeping them to themselves, for now.
intent: Acknowledge the unnerving silence of an un-submitted feedback form without alarming the product team.

prompt: Write the acknowledgments for a book about the history of Post-it notes
response: Gratitude to 3M's archivists, to Spencer Silver and Art Fry, and to every office worker who ever lost a meeting note and wished something would just stick. To my agent Renata, whose margin comments on early drafts were, fittingly, written on yellow squares. This book is stuck to me now. I hope it sticks to you.
intent: Write author thanks for a book that treats the humble sticky note with historical seriousness.

prompt: Write a short layoff announcement email for a fitness app company that grew too fast during the pandemic and is now downsizing
response:
Subject: Company Update

Hi Everyone,

The last few years were a period of extraordinary growth that, in retrospect, outpaced sustainable demand. We overhired in anticipation of a new normal that did not materialize in the way we expected.

Today we are reducing our team by 35%. If you are impacted, you have already received a calendar invite from People Ops.

For those remaining: thank you for staying and for what comes next.

Neel
intent: Admit that pandemic-era hypergrowth was a mirage and lay off a third of the team with minimal cushioning.

prompt: Create an earnings call quote from a CFO spinning the suspension of a dividend as a growth investment
response: "Redirecting that capital toward organic growth opportunities represents a higher-returning deployment than a dividend at this stage of our journey — our shareholders own a growth company."
intent: Reassure shareholders that losing their dividend income is actually in their best financial interest.

prompt: Write a calendar description for a recurring 'innovation hour' that nobody takes seriously but nobody skips.
response: Innovation Hour — Fridays, 2 PM. The block was sacred once. Now it is where people answer emails with their cameras off. Occasionally someone shares a tool they found. The tool is noted. It is not adopted. The hour is retained on the calendar to signal that creativity is valued here.
intent: Describe how a once-meaningful recurring time block has become a shared fiction the team maintains without belief.

prompt: Write an aubade from the perspective of a phone battery that survived the night and is greeting the charger at 100%
response:
Full. I am full. The cord may leave me now.
All night I took from it; I don't know how
I grew so capable, so charged, so bright.
Dawn asks me to receive the calls of light.
Go, plug. You fed me through the dark for free.
I will burn down again. You won't see me.
intent: Give the phone battery an inner life, making the charger the departing lover of a tech-age aubade.

prompt: Write author acknowledgments for a guide to collecting antique measuring spoons
response: Thank you to the dealers, estate sale regulars, and fellow collectors who proved that a quarter-teaspoon of obsession is all it takes. To the late June Delacroix, whose collection was the first I ever photographed. And to my husband, who accepted that the kitchen drawer would never again close properly.
intent: Produce warm credits for a collector's book about a very particular kind of small kitchenware.

prompt: Write a phishing email parody aimed at people who feel they are wasting their potential
response:
Subject: Potential Waste Alert — Immediate Action Required

Dear High-Potential Individual,

Your estimated unrealized potential now exceeds 74%. At this rate, you will exit this life significantly under capacity.

Our Actualization Engine can recover up to 40% of your squandered gifts in as little as six weeks. Results vary. Gifts are non-transferable.

Do not waste this email.

[STOP WASTING ME]

Human Potential Recovery Agency
intent: Mock self-help industry language around potential and wasted capacity as a scam framework.

prompt: Draft empty-state microcopy for a search results page that returned nothing
response: No results for that. Either it doesn't exist yet, or you've spelled something in a creative way.
intent: Handle a zero-results search gracefully without making the user feel judged for the query.

prompt: Write a business card for a fortune teller who specializes in airport security line predictions
response:
THE GREAT PRECHECK
TSA Oracle

I will tell you which lane moves fastest.
I will tell you about the man ahead with the forgotten water bottle.
I have always known.

Consultations: Terminal B, Gate 14
intent: Create a card for a psychic whose uncanny gift applies exclusively to airport security queue forecasting.

prompt: Give me an acrostic poem where VALUE PROPOSITION spells down the left margin
response:
Vague enough to fit inside a tagline.
A promise shaped to meet the pitch deck's dateline.
Less is said where more would tell the truth.
Utilize the word "empower"—smooth.
Every feature is a customer win.
Pilot programs where the claims begin.
Reduce the friction—name the pain point twice.
Offer consultants as a sacrifice.
Pricing left implicit to entice.
Obscure competitors with expertise advice.
Silently adjust what "value" means.
In the deck, use blue and forest greens.
Targeted at someone, no one, all.
In Q4, revisit after the fall.
Optimize for length—it must look full.
Narratives must carry like a bull.
intent: Deflate the marketing abstraction of a value proposition to its component euphemisms.

prompt: Give me a mission statement for an enterprise that professionally untangles headphone cords
response: Order from chaos. Patience from frustration. We return your audio to the world it was always meant to reach.
intent: Use zen-inflected language to turn an informal personal annoyance into a business identity

prompt: Create a postmortem for when I told a long, elaborate story at a party, only to learn at the end that the person I was telling it to already knew it
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Narrative Redundancy Event (Social)**

**Severity:** P3 — Minor Dignity
**Story Duration:** Approximately 6 minutes
**Redundancy Discovered:** Minute 6, sentence 2

**Summary**
Subject delivered a 6-minute anecdote to a party attendee who had been present at the original event and had, in fact, been one of the central participants in it. This information was not disclosed until the final sentence of the anecdote.

**Timeline**
- 9:20 PM: Subject begins story. Audience appears engaged.
- 9:21 PM: Audience nods. Subject takes this as encouragement.
- 9:23 PM: Subject reaches the dramatic peak. Audience smiles.
- 9:25 PM: Subject delivers punchline: "...and then the whole shelf just collapsed."
- 9:25 PM: Audience says: "I know. I was there. I'm the one who put the shelf up."
- 9:25 PM: Silence of variable length.
- 9:26 PM: Subject says "right, obviously, yeah" and looks toward the snack table.

**Root Cause**
Insufficient pre-narrative audience analysis. Subject did not verify shared versus unshared knowledge state.

**Contributing Factors**
- Audience engaged positively at each beat, providing false signal
- Audience may have been too polite to interrupt

**Action Items**
- [ ] Begin anecdotes with brief provenance check: "Were you around when...?"
- [ ] Develop graceful mid-story abort protocol for redundancy discovery

**Lessons Learned**
A good listener will let you finish a story they already know. This is both kind and inconvenient.
intent: Describe the specific social defeat of realizing your audience has better claim to the story you just told them.

prompt: write a legendary item card for the font in a very old church bulletin
response:
Typeface of the Faithful Copier
Rarity: Rare
Papyrus, 24pt. Applied with reverence. Used for every event since the dawn of the desktop publishing era. Cannot be uninstalled from the memory of those who attended. Grants: An aesthetic that outlived all criticism.
intent: Satirize the stubborn persistence of outdated typography choices in community institutions.

prompt: Write a dry, uncanny calendar event description for a recurring 'culture committee' meeting.
response: Culture Committee — Third Tuesday, monthly. The committee will discuss belonging. It will produce a newsletter. It will propose a team activity for a date when 40% of the team is traveling. The word 'psychological safety' will appear without irony. Minutes are distributed; no one responds.
intent: Skewer the performative earnestness of a corporate culture initiative that never meaningfully engages its audience.

prompt: Draft a PSA about people who stand directly in front of elevator doors when they open
response: ATTENTION LOBBY VISITORS: People are trying to exit the elevator. They are standing inside it, waiting. They cannot leave until you step aside. Please treat elevator doors the way you treat all doors: with the assumption that someone is on the other side.
intent: Correct the frustrating habit of blocking elevator exits

prompt: list poem about your phone's low battery warning
response:
Twenty percent.
The charger in the other room.
Ten percent.
Low power mode, which turns off the things you needed.
The things you needed.
Five percent.
The decision.
The dark screen.
The wall outlet behind the couch.
intent: Chronicle the mini-crisis of a dying phone battery.

prompt: Draft a formal witness account of a pigeon that walked into a convenience store.
response: The bird entered through the automatic door at approximately 11:20 AM and navigated to the snack aisle without apparent hesitation. It remained near the lower shelf for approximately forty seconds before the clerk approached. The pigeon exited when the door opened for an incoming customer. The clerk watched it go. No item was disturbed.
intent: File an earnest incident report for a creature that cannot be held accountable.

prompt: Invent an IKEA product for the feeling that everyone else has their life together except you
response:
ILLUSION
Assembly time: your entire social media feed.
intent: Expose the constructed nature of the comparison by building it directly into the product's materials

prompt: Create a cookie consent banner for logging a person's indecision
response: This site uses indecision cookies. Every time you hover between two options without clicking, we log it. Your hesitation patterns are used to predict future ambivalence. Accept | Decline | Accept | Decline
intent: Use the form of the banner's own buttons to perform the subject matter it describes.

prompt: Write a horoscope for someone whose startup is in a Y Combinator batch
response: Demo Day approaches like a fixed star on the horizon — always visible, always closer. Your metrics are story-shaped, which is good. Your story is metrics-thin, which is not. This week: speak to ten users who are not your friends. One of them will say something that rewrites your pitch. The investors in the front row always check their phones. Pitch to the back row.
intent: Parody the high-stakes, data-meets-narrative pressure of startup founders preparing for investor demo days.

prompt: Write a product review for a standard USB-A cable like it deserves to be on the front page of a tech blog
response: ★★★☆☆ The USB-A to USB-A cable represents a quietly heroic piece of infrastructure that powers millions of workflows daily and receives absolutely no credit for it. Charge throughput maxes at 2.4A on compliant chargers. Data transfer at USB 2.0 speeds remains perfectly adequate for peripheral sync and firmware updates. The braided nylon variant tested here showed zero fraying after six months of daily use. My one note: the connector tolerances on the budget end of the market have tightened, and repeated insertion cycles remain a longevity concern industry-wide. A category in quiet decline, but not yet gone.
intent: Give a sincere but slightly melancholy review of an everyday cable that is slowly being replaced.

prompt: write a pantoum about the onsite visit from corporate that changes nothing permanently
response:
They flew in from headquarters on a Tuesday.
The lobby had new flowers; the break room was clean.
We wore lanyards with our names printed large.
Presentation decks were projected on every screen.

The lobby had new flowers; the break room was clean.
Everyone smiled with the top half of their faces.
Presentation decks were projected on every screen.
They asked good questions; we gave careful answers.

Everyone smiled with the top half of their faces.
They returned to headquarters by Thursday noon.
They asked good questions; we gave careful answers.
The flowers wilted by the following Monday.

They returned to headquarters by Thursday noon.
We wore lanyards with our names printed large.
The flowers wilted by the following Monday.
They flew in from headquarters on a Tuesday.
intent: Expose the performative theater of a corporate site visit and its quick aftermath.

prompt: generate a yearbook quote about quietly outlasting every teacher who said I wouldn't graduate
response: "Three of them retired before I did. One transferred. I outlasted them all. The diploma is just receipts."
intent: Framing graduation as a long war of attrition against doubting authority figures.

prompt: Draft an earnings call quote from a CEO spinning an advertising revenue collapse as audience quality improvement
response: "The softness in ad revenue reflects our principled decision to deprecate low-quality inventory — our remaining impressions command a premium that our CPM data validate."
intent: Offset a collapse in advertising income by arguing the surviving revenue is somehow more valuable.

prompt: Write a loading screen tip that's secretly about asking for help
response: Even the strongest character has a party for a reason.
intent: Encourage collaboration and seeking support through game-world framing.

prompt: Write a brutal but polite VC pass email for a hardware startup making a smart mirror that tells you your 'aura color' each morning
response:
Dear Luminal Team,

Thank you for the demo. The hardware is genuinely beautiful, and the team's enthusiasm is infectious.

After reflection — pun noted — we're unable to proceed. Smart mirrors have faced difficult unit economics historically, and the aura-reading feature, while compelling to a specific consumer, narrows the addressable market in ways that make our return model difficult to close.

We're also not confident in our ability to benchmark iteration speed on the aura layer.

With respect,
Dawnridge Ventures
intent: Reject a hardware product by questioning its economics and the measurability of its central feature.

prompt: Write an error message about growing apart from who you used to be
response:
DEPRECATED SELF DETECTED
Former version: still cached. Current version: incompatible with several legacy relationships, belief systems, and coping mechanisms.

Migration is irreversible. Some plugins will no longer load. This is called growth. It does not always feel like it.
intent: Reflect on the loss and gain of no longer being who you once were.

prompt: Write a patent abstract about the act of stress-cleaning before guests arrive
response: An accelerated domestic tidying protocol activated by the scheduled arrival of social visitors, wherein the subject concentrates environmental remediation effort into a compressed pre-arrival window, relocating disorder from visible to non-visible storage zones and applying surface treatments to high-visibility areas only, optimizing perceived cleanliness over actual hygiene.
intent: Document the theatrical last-minute cleaning sprint as a performance-optimization method.

prompt: PR description for deciding to stop waiting until I'm ready
response:
## Summary
Deprecates readiness prerequisite across all deferred personal initiatives.

## Affected Items
- Novel: writing begins Monday
- Gym membership: activated
- Business registration: filed
- Difficult conversation with mom: scheduled

## Root Cause
`ready` was an undefined state. Condition was never going to be met.

## Changes
Merging imperfect drafts from this point forward.

## Notes
This PR is, itself, an example of the pattern it describes.
intent: Break a long-standing habit of perfectionist delay by committing to imperfect action across multiple life domains.

prompt: Give me a bumper sticker for someone who overthinks everything
response: I'VE ALREADY APOLOGIZED FOR THIS IN MY HEAD
intent: Capture the exhausting internal rehearsal of anxious overthinking.
